metadata.title: Iran-Israel Conflict Cost Tracker
metadata.description: Comprehensive tracking of military expenditures, economic impacts, and financial costs associated with the Iran-Israel conflict escalation of 2026.

sources.name: Congressional Budget Office (CBO)
sources.description: Baseline defense spending projections and supplemental appropriations analysis

sources.name: U.S. Department of Defense (DoD)
sources.description: Official military budget data, weapon system costs, and operational expenditure reports

sources.name: Congressional Research Service (CRS)
sources.description: Non-partisan analysis of military operations costs, foreign aid, and defense procurement

sources.name: Stockholm International Peace Research Institute (SIPRI)
sources.description: International arms transfers, military expenditure databases, and conflict cost modeling

sources.name: Brown University Costs of War Project
sources.description: Comprehensive accounting of post-9/11 war costs including long-term veteran care and interest on borrowing

sources.name: Center for Strategic and International Studies (CSIS)
sources.description: Defense program analysis and missile defense cost assessments

metadata.methodology: Cost estimates are derived from publicly available DoD budget documents, CBO scoring of supplemental appropriations, CRS unit cost analyses for weapon systems, and SIPRI military expenditure databases. Daily operational burn rates are calculated using DoD cost-per-flying-hour data, Navy ship operating cost models, and historical precedent from Operations Inherent Resolve and Prosperity Guardian. Indirect and economic costs are modeled using IMF World Economic Outlook methodologies and historical oil price shock analyses. All figures are in 2026 USD unless otherwise noted. Estimates carry an uncertainty range of approximately +/- 15%.
metadata.disclaimer: These figures are estimates compiled from multiple open-source intelligence and government reporting channels. Actual costs may differ significantly due to classified expenditures, delayed reporting, and the inherent uncertainty of ongoing military operations. This data is presented for informational and educational purposes only. The use of named weapon systems and unit costs reflects publicly available procurement data and does not represent classified operational details.

us.name: United States

munitionsExpended.description: Tomahawk cruise missiles (400+ used), JASSM-ER, GBU-57 MOPs, precision-guided munitions, Merops interceptor drones (10,000 deployed at $14K each). CSIS: $5.6B in munitions first 2 days alone. Pentagon $50B supplemental requested for replenishment.
munitionsExpended.source: DoD Comptroller / CRS Precision-Guided Munitions Report

navalOperations.description: 3 carrier strike groups (Lincoln, Ford, Bush), 2,500+ Marines + USS Tripoli deploying, destroyer/submarine operations, naval aviation sorties. 3 CSGs at $7M/day each = $21M/day combined. France also deployed Charles de Gaulle + 10 warships.
navalOperations.source: Navy Budget Office / USNI News Fleet Tracker

airOperations.description: B-2 Spirit and B-1B sorties from Whiteman AFB and Diego Garcia ($6.7M per B-2 sortie), F-35A/F-22 operations, KC-135/KC-46 tanker support (6 crew killed in KC-135 crash Day 14). Hegseth: 'highest volume of strikes yet' on Day 14-15.
airOperations.source: Air Force Cost Per Flying Hour data / CRS Air Force Budget Report

personnelDeployment.description: ~50,000 personnel deployed to CENTCOM AOR including 5,000 Marines (31st MEU + 2,500 additional), combat pay, hostile fire pay. 13 US service members KIA (7 enemy fire, 6 KC-135 crash).

logistics.description: Strategic airlift via C-17 and C-5M, sealift operations, forward operating base establishment, fuel transport, ammunition resupply, and theater logistics network

economicDisruption.description: Stock market volatility, defense industry surge costs, supply chain disruptions, and increased homeland security posture including elevated FPCON levels at all DoD installations

oilPriceImpact.description: Cost to US economy from Brent at $103.14/bbl (+41.5% from pre-war $72.9). Gas at $3.63/gal national avg (+$0.70 since war began). IEA 400M barrel + US 172M SPR barrels released. S&P 500 at 2026 lows, down 3.1% YTD.
oilPriceImpact.source: EIA Short-Term Energy Outlook / Federal Reserve analysis

tradeDisruption.description: Rerouting of commercial shipping away from Persian Gulf, increased maritime insurance premiums, delayed LNG shipments, and supply chain delays affecting US importers and exporters
tradeDisruption.source: Bureau of Economic Analysis / International Trade Administration

humanitarian.description: USAID emergency humanitarian response, refugee assistance coordination, medical evacuation support for US nationals, and embassy emergency operations in the region
humanitarian.source: USAID / State Department Bureau of Near Eastern Affairs

us.dailyBurnRateNote: CSIS estimates $891M-$1.43B/day. Pentagon told Senate first 6 days cost $11.3B ($1.88B/day). CSIS update: $16.5B through Day 12. Fortune reports ~$1B/day ongoing. European Business Magazine projects $259B over 6 months.
us.details: CSIS: $3.7B first 100 hours, $11.3B first 6 days (Pentagon to Congress), $16.5B through Day 12. Penn Wharton projects $40B-$95B direct costs, up to $210B total economic impact. Pentagon requesting $50B supplemental. Brown University: $31.35B-$33.77B total US spending connected to broader conflict since Oct 7 2023.

israel.name: Israel

munitionsExpended.description: Arrow-3 and Arrow-2 interceptors, Iron Dome Tamir interceptors, David's Sling interceptors, LORA ballistic missiles, Delilah cruise missiles, and precision-guided munitions used in strikes and air defense
munitionsExpended.source: Israeli Ministry of Defense / SIPRI Arms Transfers Database

navalOperations.description: Sa'ar 6 corvette operations, submarine deployments in Eastern Mediterranean, naval blockade support, and maritime patrol operations

airOperations.description: F-35I Adir stealth fighter sorties, F-15I Ra'am strikes in Tehran (IDF destroyed space research center and air defense factories). ~7,600 strikes in Iran and ~1,100 in Lebanon since Feb 28.
airOperations.source: Israeli Air Force / CSIS Missile Defense Project

personnelDeployment.description: Full IDF reserve mobilization of approximately 300,000 reservists, combat pay, civil defense activation, and Home Front Command operations across all districts
personnelDeployment.source: Israeli Ministry of Finance / Bank of Israel

logistics.description: Emergency resupply from US stockpiles (WRSA-I), domestic ammunition production surge, civil defense shelter operations, and critical infrastructure hardening

economicDisruption.description: GDP loss from 300,000 reserve mobilization, Ben Gurion Airport closure, tourism collapse. Bloomberg: $13B defense supplement approved, $2.9B emergency supplement. Total 2026 defense budget $34.63B (112B shekels).

oilPriceImpact.description: Israel's energy import cost surge from elevated global oil and natural gas prices, emergency fuel purchases, and Tamar/Leviathan gas platform security operations

tradeDisruption.description: Disruption to high-tech exports, closure of Haifa and Ashdod ports to commercial traffic during threat windows, canceled international business contracts, and foreign investment flight
tradeDisruption.source: Israel Export Institute / Central Bureau of Statistics

humanitarian.description: Civil defense operations, emergency medical services surge, psychological trauma support, damaged infrastructure repair, and civilian evacuation programs from northern and southern border communities

israel.dailyBurnRateNote: Estimated daily cost including missile defense operations, IAF sorties, reserve mobilization, and civil defense

iran.name: Iran

munitionsExpended.description: Ballistic missiles (Fateh-110, Emad, Sejjil), cruise missiles (Paveh, Hoveyzeh), Shahed-136 one-way attack drones, and air defense missiles including S-300PMU2 and Bavar-373 interceptors

navalOperations.description: IRGC Navy fast attack craft, mine-laying in Hormuz approaches. 50+ naval vessels sunk/damaged, 90+ total. Navy effectively destroyed per Hegseth.

airOperations.description: IRIAF defensive sorties (limited due to coalition air superiority), drone swarm operations, air defense network activation, and dispersal of remaining air assets

personnelDeployment.description: Full mobilization of IRGC ground forces, Basij militia activation of approximately 600,000 personnel, Artesh regular army deployment to border regions, and civil defense operations

logistics.description: Dispersal of military assets to hardened facilities, tunnel network operations, emergency fuel and ammunition distribution, field hospital establishment, and command-and-control reconstitution

economicDisruption.description: Destruction of critical infrastructure including Bandar Abbas port facilities, Isfahan industrial complex damage, electrical grid degradation, and telecommunications network disruption

oilPriceImpact.description: Near-total loss of oil export revenue. Kharg Island military targets destroyed (oil infrastructure spared but threatened). Chatham House projects >10% GDP decline. Iran internet at ~4% of normal for 15 days.
oilPriceImpact.source: EIA / OPEC Monthly Oil Market Report

tradeDisruption.description: Collapse of non-oil trade through damaged ports, land border crossing disruptions, sanctions tightening cutting off remaining trade partners, and rial currency collapse

humanitarian.description: Mass displacement of approximately 2.1 million civilians from strike zones, hospital system strain from casualties, food and medicine distribution disruption, and water treatment facility damage

iran.dailyBurnRateNote: Estimated daily cost including military operations, infrastructure losses, and oil revenue foregone

weaponCosts.description: Land-attack cruise missile launched from Arleigh Burke-class destroyers and Virginia-class submarines against Iranian military and nuclear facilities
weaponCosts.source: CRS Report: Navy Tomahawk Cruise Missile Program

weaponCosts.description: 30,000-lb bunker-buster bomb delivered by B-2 Spirit stealth bombers against deeply buried Iranian nuclear enrichment facilities at Fordow and Natanz
weaponCosts.source: DoD Selected Acquisition Report / Air Force Magazine

weaponCosts.description: Extended-range joint air-to-surface standoff missile launched from B-1B Lancers, F-15E Strike Eagles, and F-35A Lightning IIs against IRGC command centers and air defense sites
weaponCosts.source: CRS Report: Joint Air-to-Surface Standoff Missile

weaponCosts.description: Aegis Ballistic Missile Defense interceptor launched from destroyers to intercept Iranian medium-range ballistic missiles targeting US bases and allied positions
weaponCosts.source: CRS Report: Navy Aegis BMD Program / MDA Budget

weaponCosts.description: Extended range active missile used for anti-air warfare and terminal ballistic missile defense against Iranian cruise missile and drone salvos
weaponCosts.source: CRS Report: Navy SM-6 Missile Program

weaponCosts.description: Precision-guided 250-lb bombs delivered by F-35A and F-15E aircraft against dispersed IRGC ground targets, radar installations, and mobile missile launchers
Short-range interceptor missiles used to defend Israeli population centers against Iranian rocket and drone attacks; high intercept rate against Shahed-136 drones and short-range rockets
weaponCosts.source: CSIS Missile Defense Project / Rafael Advanced Defense Systems

weaponCosts.description: Exo-atmospheric ballistic missile defense interceptor used against Iranian Emad and Sejjil medium-range ballistic missiles; intercepts targets in space before reentry
weaponCosts.source: CSIS Missile Defense Project / Israel Missile Defense Organization

weaponCosts.description: Endo-atmospheric ballistic missile defense interceptor providing lower-tier defense against Iranian ballistic missiles that penetrate Arrow-3 engagement envelope
Medium-range interceptor bridging the gap between Iron Dome and Arrow systems; used against Iranian cruise missiles and large-caliber rockets
Delta-wing loitering munition/kamikaze drone with ~2,500km range and 50kg warhead; launched in swarms to overwhelm Israeli air defenses and strike infrastructure
Solid-fueled tactical ballistic missile with ~300km range; launched from mobile TELs against US military positions in Iraq, Bahrain, and Qatar
Maneuverable reentry vehicle ballistic missile with ~1,700km range; primary weapon used in strikes against Israeli territory from western Iran launch sites
Two-stage solid-fueled MRBM with ~2,000km range; Iran's most capable ballistic missile with reduced launch preparation time and higher speed at reentry
Ground-launched cruise missile with ~1,650km range; used alongside ballistic missiles to create complex attack profiles against Israeli and US targets
Chinese-derived anti-ship missile deployed by IRGC Navy coastal batteries and fast attack craft against coalition naval vessels in Strait of Hormuz approaches

navalDeployments.description: US Navy force posture deployed to CENTCOM AOR and Eastern Mediterranean in response to Iran-Israel conflict
navalDeployments.carrierGroupDailyCostNote: Estimated daily operating cost per carrier strike group including air wing operations, escort vessels, and logistics ships based on Navy Visibility and Management of Operating and Support Costs (VAMOSC)
navalDeployments.destroyerDailyCostNote: Estimated daily operating cost per Arleigh Burke-class guided-missile destroyer at wartime tempo
navalDeployments.estimatedDailyCostNote: Combined daily cost of 3 carrier strike groups + USS Tripoli amphibious group + support vessels. France Charles de Gaulle + 10 warships not included.
navalDeployments.totalNavalCostNote: Total naval deployment cost for the first 14 days of active conflict operations; does not include pre-conflict surge deployment costs
navalDeployments.source: Navy VAMOSC / CRS Naval Operations Budget Reports

aidPackages.name: FY2024 National Security Supplemental - Israel Component
aidPackages.description: Part of the $95 billion national security supplemental package signed into law. Includes $4 billion for Iron Dome and David's Sling replenishment, $1.2 billion for Iron Beam development, $4.4 billion for current military operations, and $9.1 billion in humanitarian aid for Gaza. Also includes $3.5 billion for advanced weapons procurement and $4.2 billion for defense articles and services.

aidPackages.name: US-Israel 10-Year MOU Annual Allocation (FY2026)
aidPackages.description: Annual military aid under the 2016 US-Israel Memorandum of Understanding signed by the Obama administration. Provides $3.3 billion in Foreign Military Financing (FMF) and $500 million in missile defense cooperation. MOU runs from FY2019 through FY2028 at $38 billion total over 10 years.
aidPackages.source: CRS Report: US Foreign Aid to Israel

aidPackages.name: Emergency Iron Dome Replenishment (FY2025)
aidPackages.description: Emergency appropriation for Iron Dome Tamir interceptor replenishment following the June 2025 Twelve-Day War. Funds accelerated production of approximately 20,000 additional Tamir interceptors and spare components for Iron Dome batteries.

aidPackages.name: FY2026 Emergency Supplemental for Israel (Pending)
aidPackages.description: Emergency supplemental appropriations request submitted to Congress on March 4, 2026. Expected to include Iron Dome and Arrow interceptor replenishment, precision-guided munitions resupply from WRSA-I drawdown, F-35I replacement parts, and humanitarian assistance. CBO preliminary score pending. Estimates range from $12-18 billion based on historical precedent and current operational tempo.

globalGDPImpact.description: Estimated reduction in global GDP growth for 2026 due to oil price shock, trade disruption, and financial market volatility caused by the conflict and Strait of Hormuz blockage
globalGDPImpact.source: IMF World Economic Outlook methodology / historical oil shock modeling

oilPriceShock.estimatedGlobalCostNote: Annualized additional cost to global economy if prices remain elevated, based on approximately 100 million barrels/day global consumption

hormuzFlowDisruption.description: Approximately 21 million barrels per day normally transit the Strait of Hormuz (~21% of global oil consumption). Iranian mine-laying and IRGC Navy operations have reduced flow to approximately 3.5 million barrels per day through limited convoy routes.

tradeDisruption.globalTradeImpactUnit: percentage reduction in global trade volume
tradeDisruption.affectedTradeVolumeNote: Annual value of trade normally transiting the Strait of Hormuz beyond oil (LNG, petrochemicals, containerized goods)
tradeDisruption.description: Major disruption to global trade routes through Persian Gulf. LNG shipments from Qatar severely impacted. Container shipping rerouted around Cape of Good Hope. Middle East-Asia trade corridors experiencing 2-3 week delays.
tradeDisruption.source: World Trade Organization / UNCTAD Review of Maritime Transport

shippingInsuranceSurge.affectedArea: Persian Gulf, Gulf of Oman, Strait of Hormuz, Arabian Sea (north of 20°N)
shippingInsuranceSurge.description: War risk insurance premiums for vessels transiting the Persian Gulf have surged from 0.05% to 5% of hull value. A typical VLCC (Very Large Crude Carrier) valued at $120 million now faces $6 million in war risk premiums per transit compared to $60,000 pre-conflict. Many underwriters have suspended coverage entirely.
shippingInsuranceSurge.source: Lloyd's Market Association Joint War Committee

economicImpact.stockMarketImpact: S&P 500 at 6,632 — 2026 closing low, down 3.1% YTD. Third straight losing week for S&P, Nasdaq, and Dow. $3.5T wiped from global markets in first week. GDP Q4 2025 revised down to 0.7%. Consumer sentiment at 55.5 (lowest of 2026). 56% of Americans oppose the war.
economicImpact.oilPriceImpact: Brent $103.14/bbl (+41.5% from pre-war). WTI $98.71 (+47%). Fourth consecutive weekly gain. IEA 400M barrel + US 172M SPR release failing to suppress prices. Brent above $100 for two consecutive sessions. Rystad warns $135 if conflict persists 4 months.
economicImpact.gasPrice: $3.63/gallon national average (22-month high, up 70¢ since war began from $2.93). California $5.37/gal. Up 35¢ in one week alone. Polymarket: 64% odds of $4.50 by end of March.

keyFacts.fact: The US has expended approximately 400 Tomahawk cruise missiles in the first week of operations, representing roughly 25% of the Navy's estimated active inventory and requiring 2-3 years of production to replace at current manufacturing rates.
keyFacts.source: CRS Report: Navy Tomahawk Cruise Missile Program

keyFacts.fact: Israel's multi-layered missile defense system (Iron Dome, David's Sling, Arrow-2, Arrow-3) has intercepted over 85% of incoming threats, but at a combined cost exceeding $242 million in interceptor missiles alone during the first 7 days.

Each B-2 Spirit stealth bomber sortie from Whiteman AFB, Missouri to Iranian targets and back costs approximately $6.7 million in fuel, maintenance, and munitions, not including the GBU-57 MOPs which add $3.5 million each.
keyFacts.source: Air Force Cost Per Flying Hour Reports / GAO

keyFacts.fact: The Strait of Hormuz blockage is costing the global economy an estimated $3.5 billion per day in disrupted oil flows, elevated energy prices, rerouted shipping, and cascading supply chain impacts across Asia and Europe.

Iran's 500+ Shahed-136 drones cost approximately $10 million total to produce -- less than the cost of a single SM-3 interceptor ($12M) used to shoot down a ballistic missile, highlighting the asymmetric cost advantage of drone warfare.

The combined US military aid to Israel since FY2024, including the 10-year MOU, supplemental packages, and emergency appropriations, now exceeds $31 billion -- approaching the annual GDP of Jordan ($33B) and more than the entire GDP of Iceland ($28B).
keyFacts.source: CRS Report: US Foreign Aid to Israel

keyFacts.fact: Biggest weekly gain in crude oil futures history -- WTI up 35.1% and Brent up 41.6% from pre-war levels, surpassing the 1990 Gulf War spike and 2022 Russia-Ukraine oil shock.
VLCC (Very Large Crude Carrier) charter rates surged from $50,000/day to $400,000/day -- an 8x increase -- as vessel supply collapsed due to the Hormuz blockade and insurance withdrawal.

CSIS estimates the war cost the US $16.5 billion through Day 12 — an average of $1.375 billion per day. The Pentagon told senators the first 6 days alone cost $11.3 billion, with $5.6 billion spent on munitions in just the first 48 hours.

10,000 AI-powered Merops interceptor drones were deployed to the Middle East in under 5 days — each costs $14,000-$15,000 compared to $4M+ for a Patriot interceptor. The entire deployment cost roughly $150M, replacing billions in traditional air defense expenditure.

totals.description: Combined direct military (33.1B) + indirect economic costs (113.0B) for all three belligerents through Day 15. CSIS benchmark: $16.5B US costs through Day 12.
